analyses.video_title: Should You Buy Micron Stock Before the Huge Investor Update? | MU Stock Analysis
analyses.summary:
Parkev Tatevosian addresses a common viewer question ahead of Micron's June 24, 2026 earnings report: should you buy MU stock now or wait? He begins by recapping the company's extraordinary recent performance — Micron's stock is up over 775% in the past year — and reaffirming his long-standing buy thesis, which he first published over a year ago. The catalyst has been surging AI-driven demand from data centers at a time when industry supply remains constrained, creating a pricing environment where Micron holds significant leverage.

The recent quarterly numbers are striking. Revenue came in at $23.9 billion, nearly tripling year-over-year. Gross margins expanded to 74.4% and operating margins to 67.6% — figures Parkev notes are approaching software-company territory for a manufacturer that produces physical chips entirely in-house. Free cash flow was $7 billion after $5 billion in capital expenditure. Looking ahead, management guided Q3 revenue of $33.5 billion (a ~50% sequential jump) and gross margins climbing to an unprecedented 81%.

Despite the bullish fundamental picture, Parkev raises a structural concern: semiconductors are a cyclical industry, and current peak margins make forward multiples look deceptively cheap. At 11x forward earnings and 12x forward operating cash flow — roughly half the S&P 500 average — the stock screens inexpensively, but those multiples are built on record-high denominators. He also flags a subtle management signal: with the stock near all-time highs, the team chose to pay down $4.6 billion in debt rather than execute meaningful buybacks, suggesting insiders see the valuation as stretched.

Parkev updated his DCF model to an intrinsic value of $915 per share against a market price of $1,054, placing the stock above his margin of safety threshold. He maintains a buy rating but explicitly advises against purchasing before earnings given the heightened volatility event risk and his view that downside exceeds upside at current prices after an 800% run. His recommendation: wait for the results and the post-earnings price reaction before adding exposure.

analyses.video_title: RocketLab Stock: My Final Verdict (Buy or Sell) | RKLB Stock Deep Dive Part 6
analyses.summary:
In the sixth and final installment of his Rocket Lab deep dive, Parkev Tatevosian examines the company's cash flow situation and delivers his investment verdict. He notes that RKLB burned roughly $75 million in free cash flow during the most recent quarter — $50 million in operating cash outflows plus $27 million in capital expenditure — and expects that elevated burn rate to persist through Neutron's first launch. Despite the losses, management sits on $1.5 billion in cash and equivalents, giving the company roughly 15 quarters of runway even at peak burn levels, and total liquidity exceeds $2 billion when including available credit facilities.

Parkev highlights that management has been opportunistically issuing equity — raising around $450 million in the latest quarter alone — while the stock has surged over 300% in the past year. He frames this favorably: each capital raise has occurred at higher prices as the company hits milestones, reducing the long-term cost of capital. For Q2 2026, management guided revenue of $225–240 million (roughly 16% above Q1's $200 million) with gross margins improving to around 34%.

The discussion then pivots to valuation in the context of SpaceX's anticipated IPO the following day. Parkev estimates SpaceX would price at a trailing price-to-sales near 90–95 at a $1.77 trillion valuation. By comparison, Rocket Lab trades at a forward P/S of approximately 62 — about two-thirds of SpaceX's multiple. He argues this relative discount is deserved given that Rocket Lab is a pure-play space company, whereas SpaceX is a conglomerate that now includes XAI (an AI company with excess computing capacity and modest customer traction) and a social media business — both of which traditionally command far lower multiples on their own.

Despite finding Rocket Lab's strategic position compelling and its valuation more attractive than SpaceX's, Parkev concludes the current price-to-sales of 62 is still too rich for him to rate the stock a buy. He speculates the SpaceX IPO could serve as a sentiment catalyst in either direction and suggests waiting for a more attractive entry point before committing capital.

stocks.thesis: Compelling pure-play space company with a strong cash runway and improving unit economics, but the current forward P/S of ~62 is too elevated relative to the risk profile; Parkev wants a lower entry point before upgrading to buy.

analyses.video_title: Alert: This Is How Every Market Top Begins
analyses.summary:
Gareth delivers a market-top warning, arguing that the classic two-step pattern for major tops — a lower low followed by a confirmed lower high — is now developing in the S&P 500 and NASDAQ. While the indexes remain near all-time highs (NASDAQ 100 roughly 1-1.5% off), the internal breadth has deteriorated dramatically: mega-caps like Google, Amazon, Microsoft, Netflix, and Meta are all down meaningfully on the day, while only the narrow chip AI trade is holding up.

His key framework: a market top requires first a confirmed lower low (already achieved), then a confirmed lower high. The S&P last week put in what looks like a lower high, but it hasn't been confirmed yet because the prior low hasn't been retested. He emphasises that the difference between 'bullish consolidation' and a 'market top' hinges on whether that lower high holds after the next pullback tests the prior low.

On individual names, Gareth shows that Google, Microsoft, and Meta are already in clear lower-high/lower-low downtrends, with institutional sellers entering on each bounce. He uses these charts to illustrate that while the index headline numbers look benign, the underlying stocks driving past rallies are structurally weakening. Financial stocks are singled out as a pocket of relative strength, benefiting from a higher-for-longer rate environment under the new Fed chair Kevin Warsh.

On SpaceX, Gareth takes a bearish stance. The volume-weighted average price is approximately $181, meaning the average buyer is underwater. He sees a scenario where the stock bounces and triggers selling pressure from anyone who bought between $200 and $228 trying to break even. If SpaceX breaks below its IPO-day low of $149-150, every post-IPO buyer would be negative, removing any remaining buy-the-dip support.

analyses.video_title: Nu Holdings Stock Analysis: Buy or Sell? | NU Stock Analysis
analyses.summary:
Parkev Tatevosian revisits Nu Holdings after a painful year-to-date decline of approximately 24%, addressing whether the pullback constitutes a buying opportunity. He opens by contextualizing the underperformance: multiple investment-bank downgrades from Bank of America (to underperform), Citigroup, and Susquehanna have pressured the stock, each citing leadership changes and mounting credit concerns driven by rising 30-, 60-, and 90-day loan delinquency rates.

Parkev emphasizes that defaults in Latin American consumer lending carry a higher loss-given-default than secured lending categories because there is limited collateral to recover. He notes, however, that management retains meaningful levers — curtailing new loan origination or selling debt to third parties — to manage cash flows if conditions deteriorate further.

On the positive side, the company has maintained an operating-cash-flow-to-sales ratio around 41% consistently for four years, and the forward price-to-earnings multiple has compressed to roughly 13.9x, near multi-year lows. His discounted cash flow model places fair value near $23.70 against a current price around $12.66, suggesting the stock trades at a deep discount. He acknowledges the inherent difficulty in valuing lending businesses but reiterates a buy rating, which he formally reasserted on June 16, 2026.

Parkev also flags a macro headwind: investor attention in emerging markets has rotated toward Southeast Asian semiconductor companies tied to the AI wave, leaving Latin American fintech comparatively neglected. He views this sentiment discount as part of what creates the opportunity.

stocks.thesis: Deep valuation discount (forward P/E ~13.9, DCF fair value ~$23.70 vs $12.66 price) makes the dip a buying opportunity despite legitimate credit quality concerns and bank downgrades.

analyses.summary:
New Money's creator breaks down the anatomy of Elon Musk's newly achieved trillion-dollar net worth following SpaceX's IPO on June 12, 2026. He explains that the wealth is concentrated almost entirely in two companies: SpaceX (approximately 42-43% economic stake, valued at roughly $850 billion post-IPO listing and constituting about 75% of total net worth) and Tesla (approximately 20% economic interest worth roughly $280 billion, including options from the landmark 2018 compensation package reinstated by the Delaware Supreme Court in December 2025).

The creator methodically addresses why this trillion-dollar figure is largely inaccessible in practice. First, any meaningful liquidation would create selling pressure and signal a loss of founder confidence, depressing the very asset being sold. Second, SpaceX's IPO imposes a 366-day lock-up period through approximately June 13, 2027, legally barring Musk from selling the shares. Third, nearly 75% of the portfolio sits in a single recently-listed company — textbook concentration risk.

He then reveals the workaround used by ultra-high-net-worth individuals: pledging shares as collateral to borrow cash, avoiding both a taxable event and market selling pressure — though at the risk of margin calls in a downturn. The creator closes with the thesis that for Musk, the endgame was never liquidity — it is voting control over SpaceX and Tesla to pursue his long-term missions including a Mars colony, which is literally a condition for some of his SpaceX performance-share vesting tranches.

The creator is explicitly skeptical of the SpaceX valuation, stating he considers it massively overvalued and places his own fair value around $440 billion versus the $1.75 trillion+ IPO price, suggesting Musk's true net worth could be far lower if market sentiment corrects.

analyses.video_title: This Chart Lines Up Eerily With 1929, Great Depression Coming?
analyses.summary:
Gareth Soloway presents a macro bear case built around a 100-year cycle comparison between the 1920s and today. He argues that multiple structural parallels — post-pandemic boom, transformative technology euphoria, retail speculation, elite information weaponization, circular capital flows among mega-caps, and extreme market concentration — closely mirror the conditions of the late 1920s ahead of the 1929 crash and Great Depression.

Soloway draws specific parallels: the Spanish flu of 1918 maps to COVID-19 in 2020 as the demand catalyst; electrification and the automobile map to AI and cloud infrastructure; bucket shops and margin speculation map to zero-day options and prediction markets; and 1920s utility holding companies recycling capital internally maps to today's mega-cap tech firms investing in AI startups that immediately route funds back to buy cloud and chips from the same parent companies.

His chart analysis centers on the Dow Jones logarithmic chart, where he identifies a long-term parallel channel. He notes the market currently sits above the upper band of this channel — the same position it was in just before the 1929 crash. Using a date-range measurement, he calculates that approximately 3,000 days elapsed from the post-Spanish flu bull market start to the 1929 top. Applying the same 3,000-day window from the COVID low in 2020 projects a potential market top around mid-to-late 2028, leading into a 2029 crash — the 100-year anniversary. He is explicit that this is speculative and markets could see further 20–30% corrections along the way before any final top.

Soloway stops short of making any specific stock recommendations. The video is a macro warning framework, not a trade idea, and he acknowledges the scenario may not materialize.
key_theses: The current market environment has striking structural similarities to the late 1920s — post-pandemic boom, transformative tech, retail speculation, and extreme index concentration
A logarithmic Dow chart shows the market above its long-term upper channel band, the same position it occupied just before the 1929 crash
Date-range analysis from COVID low (2020) projects a potential cyclical top around mid-to-late 2028, followed by a 2029 crash — the 100-year cycle
Extreme concentration in 10–15 AI-related stocks masks underlying market weakness; advance-decline breadth is poor
US debt trajectory ($4T+ annual additions, compounding) becomes unsustainable by the 2028–2029 window
warnings_or_risks: Gareth is explicit this is speculative — the cycle may not materialize or could arrive later
Markets can continue rallying 20–30% from here before any major top

Zero-day options and retail prediction platforms have increased systemic fragility relative to historical norms
Federal Reserve money printing and government deficit spending may delay but ultimately worsen the eventual correction

analyses.video_title: Should You Buy Nike Stock Before the Huge Investor Update? | NKE Stock Analysis
analyses.summary:
Parkev delivers a cautious pre-earnings take on Nike ahead of its June 30th quarterly report, concluding he would not buy the stock before the results. Nike is down approximately 29% year-to-date in 2026, and Parkev says he had warned this would happen given the headwinds and overvaluation entering the year.

The most recent quarter (ended February 28) showed flat revenue at $11.3 billion, down 3% on a currency-neutral basis — a stark contrast to Nike's historical high single-digit to low double-digit growth. Gross margins fell 130 basis points to 40.2%, and EBIT margins declined to 5.6% for the quarter from 7.3% a year prior, with the nine-month figure also materially worse at 7.1% versus 9.9%. Tariffs and rising trade barriers are the primary culprit for margin compression.

Current CEO Elliott Hill's wholesale rebuilding strategy is showing only nascent progress. Wholesale revenues grew 5% (1% currency-neutral) to $6.5 billion, but Nike Direct suffered — digital down 9% and owned stores down 5%. Parkev acknowledges the strategy direction is sound but questions whether it's working fast enough, flagging the risk of another strategic pivot in coming quarters if momentum doesn't accelerate.

On valuation, Parkev's revised DCF places Nike's fair value at $36.21, well below the current ~$45 price, making the stock overvalued even after a 29% decline. He explicitly says he wants to own Nike but not at these prices, and is waiting either for a meaningfully lower price or for evidence the turnaround is gaining traction.
macro_topics: Tariff and trade barrier impact on apparel margins

stocks.thesis: Nike's fundamentals have deteriorated faster than its stock price has fallen — EBIT margins at multi-year lows, revenue declining, and DCF fair value at $36.21 versus a $45 market price makes this a stock to watch but not buy ahead of earnings.

analyses.summary:
Gareth Soloway delivers a technical analysis sweep of the major crypto markets, combining chart pattern reading with Elliott Wave concepts. His overarching view is cautiously bearish on Bitcoin in the medium term, with selective bullishness on specific altcoins showing relative strength.

On Bitcoin, Gareth is concerned about a breakout level that was reclaimed but subsequently broken back below, which he likens to a door being knocked off its hinges — the psychological and technical damage lingers even after price recovers above the level. He expects Bitcoin to need one more meaningful leg down to flush out the weak hands, particularly noting that Michael Saylor's average cost in the low $70,000s represents a magnet for a potential squeeze. That said, he is not outright bearish for swing traders and has maintained profitable long positions throughout the year.

Solana is his top altcoin pick. He highlights the asset's long-term ascending trend line dating to 2024 as a reliable support level that has held on multiple tests, and notes exceptional relative strength versus both Bitcoin and the broader altcoin market in recent sessions. He targets a move to $83 near-term and a retest of the $97–100 zone on a broader crypto rally.

XRP presents an interesting technical setup — a well-defined descending trend line that has been tested six times. The more times a trend line is tested without breaking, the more explosive the eventual breakout tends to be. Gareth is neutral-to-cautiously optimistic; support at $1 defines the risk. Ethereum is holding its key support line better than Bitcoin. Avalanche needs to reclaim $6.38 on a daily close before becoming actionable. Sui (SUI) at ~$0.71 with a stop at $0.66 offers a defined 7% risk for potential 10-40% upside. Hyperliquid shows a potential bear-flag formation.

analyses.video_title: Is RocketLab Stock Financially Viable? | RKLB Stock Deep Dive Part 5
analyses.summary:
Part five of Parkev's Rocket Lab deep dive shifts from innovation narrative to financial fundamentals. He breaks down revenue by segment: Space Systems generated $137M in the most recent quarter (up 57% YoY, 32% QoQ), while Launch Services contributed $63.7M (up 79% YoY but down 16% QoQ due to fewer launches in the period). Space Systems is the larger revenue contributor today, but Launch Services is growing faster and generating more contract value — pointing to where the long-term business mix is heading.

The company ended Q1 with approximately $2.2B in total backlog, split 41.5% Launch and 58.5% Space Systems. Management expects roughly 36% of that backlog to convert to revenue within the next 12 months — approximately $800M — which aligns with a run-rate of about $200M/quarter. Parkev frames this conversion visibility as a meaningful de-risking factor for near-term revenue. Headcount in production roles grew by 250 to ~1,450, largely from personnel moving from R&D into production, signaling the transition from development to commercial scale.

Parkev is candid that Rocket Lab is early stage and that financial analysis is secondary to the innovation story at this point. He frames the investment case as a potential 5x, 10x, or even 100x opportunity given the vast and difficult-to-quantify upside of the space economy. The video closes as a teaser for a final part of the series, with the valuation verdict presumably coming next.

macro_topics.description: The commercial space industry is in an early expansion phase with revenue opportunities that are genuinely difficult to forecast. Launch services, satellite platforms, and space systems components are all scaling simultaneously as demand from government and commercial customers accelerates.

macro_topics.title: Defense and Government Space Spending
macro_topics.description: Rocket Lab's backlog is diversified across government and commercial programs. The European defense spending surge and US government satellite programs are structural drivers of launch and space systems demand that extend well beyond near-term earnings visibility.

macro_topics.title: Reusable Launch Vehicles
macro_topics.description: The economics of the launch business are being fundamentally reshaped by reusability. Rocket Lab's progress toward reusable Neutron launches is the key swing factor in determining long-term unit economics and the addressable market size for launch services.

analyses.video_title: Adobe Stock: My Final Verdict: (Buy or Sell) | ADBE Stock Deep Dive Part 5
analyses.summary:
In the concluding part of his five-part Adobe deep dive, Parkev delivers a strongly bullish verdict, calling the stock 'ridiculously undervalued' at ~$207 per share against his DCF intrinsic value of $370. He is an existing shareholder and states he is considering adding more shares in the coming weeks.

Parkev reviews the most recently completed quarter: ending ARR grew 12.5% to $27 billion (including ~$480M from the Semrush acquisition), remaining performance obligations rose 13% year-over-year to $22.2 billion, and cash flow from operations came in at $2.17 billion (~30-33% CFO-to-sales margin). He highlights that enterprise customers spending over $10 million annually with Adobe grew more than 20% — nearly double the overall revenue growth rate — as evidence that the most important cohort is not defecting.

He acknowledges three concerns: the CFO departure, a reduction in the full-year ending ARR guidance even after an acquisition (lowered to 10% growth), and pushed-back price increases signaling near-term pricing power erosion from free AI-driven competitors. Despite these, he argues the 45% operating margin target for 2026 gives Adobe ample financial runway to experiment with freemium without destabilizing the core business.

Valuation is the centrepiece of his thesis. At less than 8x forward P/E and less than 8x forward price-to-operating-cash-flow, Parkev says the market is pricing Adobe as a zero-growth company — which he considers absurd for a business growing revenue at double digits with an opportunity to re-accelerate to mid-teens. He characterises 2026 as a year of accumulation, expecting the catalyst (two consecutive quarters of revenue acceleration) to take at least 6-9 months to materialise.

analyses.video_title: Get Ready 🚀 Deep dive Hottest Stock on Wall Street
analyses.summary:
Josh and guest Ashley (Trading with Ashley) deliver a comprehensive bullish case for memory chip stocks ahead of Micron's earnings on Wednesday. The core thesis is that memory — particularly HBM for AI inference — is the new dominant AI theme of 2026, displacing Nvidia's prior monopoly on investor attention. Both hosts view any pullback as a buying opportunity rather than a reason to exit.

On Micron specifically, the fundamentals case centres on explosive earnings growth expectations (93% next year per Yahoo Finance estimates), a PEG ratio below 1 despite a ~53 P/E, and direct pricing power driven by AI companies' insatiable demand for high-bandwidth memory. Ashley explains that AI models' ability to maintain context is directly limited by memory capacity — framing memory as the scarce bottleneck in the AI value chain. The technical picture shows MU already broke out above its prior all-time high of $1,089 ahead of earnings, and Josh expects the stock could reach $1,200 on the opening or during Monday/Tuesday before the Wednesday earnings event. He also acknowledges significant post-earnings pullback risk, using SanDisk's post-earnings sell-off followed by a near-100% recovery as a precedent.

On DRRAM (a blended memory ETF), Josh identifies $73 as the critical battleground: a weekly close above $73 sets up a run to $84, while a break below targets $64. The ETF is presented as a lower-priced, diversified alternative for retail investors. Ashley details her options strategy — selling cash-secured puts at key technical levels to get paid to wait for the entry price she wants, and buying long-dated LEAPS on confirmed pullbacks to key EMAs.

Both hosts frame the broader market as still in an AI-driven bull cycle but acknowledge two bearish stacking factors: historical average 12% pullback when there is a new Fed chair, and historical average 19.9% pullback in midterm election years. Their net view is that memory is the sector to own through any volatility — 'the single set of stocks worth buying the red on in 2026.'

analyses.video_title: Should Investors Buy Chipotle Stock on the Dip? | CMG Stock Analysis
analyses.summary:
Parkev revisits Chipotle following an 11% year-to-date decline in 2026 and concludes the dip represents a buying opportunity. He attributes the underperformance to a combination of macro headwinds — industry-wide restaurant traffic declines and rising input costs — and company-specific factors including mixed analyst ratings (J.P. Morgan upgraded to overweight while Morgan Stanley downgraded to equal weight) and the lingering sentiment impact of losing star CEO Brian Niccol to Starbucks.

On the positive side, Chipotle beat first-quarter 2026 expectations with 7.4% year-over-year revenue growth and is accelerating its unit expansion from ~200 per year to 350 locations in 2026, targeting over 7,000 total US and Canada locations. Parkev frames the company's long-term unit growth story as a core thesis that has not changed.

The main concern is a trend reversal in profitability. Operating cash flow to sales peaked at 18.6% in 2024 and dipped to 17.7% in 2025. Operating margin similarly peaked at 16.9% in 2024 and slipped to 16.2% in 2025. While the absolute decline is modest, Parkev notes that investors had long paid a premium valuation specifically because of relentless margin expansion, and any reversal erodes that multiple.

Valuation is mixed. On a forward P/E basis (~27.4x) and forward price-to-operating-cash-flow (~20x), the stock is at its cheapest level in years. However, Parkev's DCF calculates a fair value of $26.51 versus the current price of $33, suggesting slight overvaluation on an intrinsic basis. Despite this, he reiterated a buy rating on June 16, 2026, arguing the margin headwinds are industry-wide rather than Chipotle-specific.

analyses.video_title: Adobe Stock Investors are Skeptical About the Freemium Strategy | Adobe Stock Deep Dive Part 4
analyses.summary:
In the fourth installment of his Adobe deep dive, Parkev Tatevosian examines the company's pivot toward a freemium acquisition model and its implications for near-term revenue and long-term growth. Adobe management has explicitly acknowledged that the shift will weigh on short-term annual recurring revenue (ARR) while accelerating user acquisition and monthly active users, with the intent of building lifetime customer value by removing friction from onboarding.

A centerpiece of the freemium push is the Productivity Agent embedded in Acrobat — an AI tool that draws on document intelligence to create presentations, podcasts, and social content from PDFs, and enables conversational editing within the document itself. Parkev notes this is a logical move: users already extract PDF content into external AI platforms, and Adobe is simply offering that workflow natively. AI Acrobat Assistant paid monthly active users grew 150% year-over-year in the most recent quarter, and lifetime AI users in Acrobat tripled year-over-year.

Despite these metrics, Parkev acknowledges investor skepticism: the AI user base remains a small fraction of overall ARR, insufficient to move the aggregate revenue needle meaningfully. He frames the early results as directionally encouraging — freemium creative monthly active users expanded from just over 50 million to over 90 million, and adobe.com traffic for content-creation categories rose more than 50% — but notes the critical unresolved question is whether Adobe can convert that engagement into monetization at scale.

His overall stance is cautiously optimistic: Adobe's strategy is reasonable, the early data is promising, and the current stock price is relatively depressed. If monetization follows user adoption, he expects a significant re-rating of the shares.

stocks.thesis: Adobe's freemium pivot is strategically sound and early AI adoption metrics are strong, but monetization conversion remains unproven; if it works, the stock at relatively low levels has significant upside.

analyses.video_title: Celsius Stock Analysis: My Final Verdict (Buy or Sell) | CELH Stock Deep Dive Part 5
analyses.summary:
This is the concluding part of Parkev's five-part Celsius deep dive, covering growth opportunities and delivering a final buy/sell verdict. He opens by highlighting a structural retail tailwind: store managers are actively expanding energy drink shelf space — and specifically the premium cold section — as the category grows as a percentage of total beverage sales. Cold placement drives impulse purchases, making it a durable demand driver beyond just the drinks themselves.

On financials, Parkev walks through Wall Street consensus estimates: analysts project 33% revenue growth in fiscal 2026 (boosted by the Alani Nu and A-Shoc acquisitions), normalizing to roughly 10% in 2027 and 7% in 2028. He acknowledges near-term margin compression from tariffs and Iranian conflict-related commodity cost increases, but notes management's confidence in returning gross margins above 50% and operating margins above 20%.

For valuation, Parkev uses two methods. On a market multiples basis, the forward P/E of 17.8x and forward price-to-OCF of 16x are both the lowest since January 2024 — suggesting slight undervaluation relative to peers and history. On a DCF basis, he models free cash flow growing from $400M in 2026 to over $2B by 2035, using an 11.5% WACC (cost of equity 12%, beta 1.25, risk-free rate 4.5%). This produces a fair value of approximately $58/share against a current price near the 52-week low of $28.26 — roughly 100% upside.

His final verdict is a clear buy: the stock is meaningfully undervalued on DCF and at least fairly valued on multiples, with intact long-term growth drivers (international expansion, shelf space gains, margin recovery as acquisition costs fade) providing multi-year runway.

macro_topics.title: Energy Drink Market Growth
macro_topics.description: Energy drinks are taking a rising share of total beverages consumed globally, and retailers are allocating more premium cold shelf space to the category. Higher average selling prices relative to standard beverages make the category attractive for retailers, and cold placement drives impulse purchases — a structural tailwind for leading brands.

macro_topics.title: Tariff and Commodity Cost Pressure
macro_topics.description: The Iran conflict and Strait of Hormuz closure have elevated oil and commodity prices, feeding through to input costs for consumer brands. Celsius is experiencing near-term gross margin compression as a result, though management expects recovery above 50% gross margins once these pressures ease.

macro_topics.title: Consumer Staples Acquisitions
macro_topics.description: Celsius completed acquisitions of Alani Nu and A-Shoc, which inflate reported revenue growth to 33% in fiscal 2026 before normalizing to single digits in 2027–2028. M&A integration costs are depressing near-term margins but expanding total addressable market and distribution reach.

macro_topics.title: Buyback Programs as Value Signal
macro_topics.description: Management is repurchasing shares at prices well below Parkev's calculated fair value of $58. When insiders buy at a significant discount to intrinsic value, it signals confidence in the business trajectory and provides a floor of support for the stock price.

stocks.thesis: Celsius is meaningfully undervalued on a DCF basis (fair value ~$58 vs. ~$28 current price) and at the cheapest market multiples in over two years, while retaining intact long-term growth drivers in international expansion, shelf space gains, and margin recovery.

analyses.summary:
Daniel Pronk presents five stocks he is actively buying, framing all of them as undervalued relative to their fundamental growth. The video is driven by recent news catalysts and updated DCF models across each name.

Amazon leads the list. Pronk argues the market is overlooking Amazon's chips business, which Andy Jassy disclosed has an annualized revenue run rate of $20 billion growing triple digits year-over-year, with a $50 billion potential if sold externally — which would make it larger than AMD today. Amazon is also exploring selling Trainium chips directly to third parties, a major potential monetization unlock. Despite this news, the stock has sold off roughly 11% in the past month. Pronk's conservative DCF (16% operating cash flow growth, 20x multiple) yields a $309 fair value and a 19% three-year CAGR. He has been buying heavily and it is one of his largest positions.

Meta is his second pick. Pronk acknowledges a swirl of negative sentiment — the CTO publicly flagged employee morale at a 20-year low following repeated layoff waves — but argues the underlying business is accelerating anyway (30%+ revenue growth, record operating cash flow), making morale a short-term noise factor. At 18x forward earnings and 11.8x forward operating cash flow, both well below historical averages and below the S&P 500, he views the stock as pricing in the risks already. He has been adding shares.

Mercado Libre is Pronk's third pick and one he has held since $1,800 per share (now ~$1,600, so he is down). He remains highly convicted, citing a new investor presentation showing 49% revenue CAGR since 2020, 73% advertising growth acceleration, a YouTube partnership for in-video commerce in Mexico and Argentina, and a string of recent insider buys — the first since 2022. He views the 40% correction from all-time highs as an opportunity and has been increasing his position significantly.

Brookfield Asset Management is his fourth pick, described as a dividend-growth compounder. Management is projecting 20% earnings growth through 2030 and 15% annual dividend growth on top of a current 4%+ yield. Pronk's DCF (15% earnings growth, 25x PE) generates a ~16% CAGR, which he views as conservative given the company's historical trading multiple closer to 30x.

Constellation Software rounds out the list. Pronk highlights that despite a 50%+ correction from highs, the business is still growing 20%+ annually and now trades at only 15x free cash flow. AI has not caused customer churn; instead customers are coming to Constellation to build new AI features on top of existing software. He also mentions buying Topicus and Signity (a Polish subsidiary growing 40% at 12x cash flows) as extensions of the same thesis.

analyses.video_title: Massive News for Adobe Stock Investors! | ADBE Stock Deep Dive Part 3
analyses.summary:
In Part 3 of his Adobe deep dive, Parkev Tatevosian focuses on leadership transitions at the company and what they mean for the freemium strategy Adobe has recently adopted. The news catalyst is the CFO's announced resignation to become CEO of Marvell, with a 20-year Adobe veteran named as interim CFO. Separately, the sitting CEO is transitioning to chairman, meaning Adobe is simultaneously searching for both a new CEO and has recently lost its CFO — an unusual situation that coincides with a beat-and-raise quarter and raised fiscal 2026 revenue and EPS guidance.

Parkev's central concern is the strategic uncertainty this creates. Adobe is mid-pivot to a freemium model designed to convert high traffic volumes into paying customers. However, management transitions at this scale typically make incoming leaders hesitant to inherit a fixed strategy they didn't design. If the board constrains the new CEO to continue the freemium path, it makes the role less attractive to strong candidates. If the new CEO gets full discretion, the current strategy may be abandoned. Either outcome introduces meaningful uncertainty.

On the underlying business, Parkev notes that Adobe is seeing impressive demand signals — website traffic grew 40% year-over-year — but the company has so far struggled to convert free users into paying subscribers. He draws an analogy to Nike's failed direct-to-consumer pivot as a cautionary example of a strategy that sounded compelling but destroyed value in practice. That said, he acknowledges Adobe's structural strengths: nearly a billion monthly active users across its suite and significant cash generation that gives management room to experiment.

Parkev does not deliver a buy or sell rating in this video, framing it as part three of an ongoing series. The tone is cautious — he is watching whether the conversion from free to paid accelerates before committing to a bullish view.

Space Economy Growth
macro_topics.description: The commercial space industry is in an early expansion phase with revenue opportunities that are genuinely difficult to forecast. Launch services, satellite platforms, and space systems components are all scaling simultaneously as demand from government and commercial customers accelerates.

macro_topics.title: Defense and Government Space Spending
macro_topics.description: Rocket Lab's backlog is diversified across government and commercial programs. The European defense spending surge and US government satellite programs are structural drivers of launch and space systems demand that extend well beyond near-term earnings visibility.

macro_topics.title: Reusable Launch Vehicles
macro_topics.description: The economics of the launch business are being fundamentally reshaped by reusability. Rocket Lab's progress toward reusable Neutron launches is the key swing factor in determining long-term unit economics and the addressable market size for launch services.

macro_topics.title: Pricing power erosion from free AI tools
CEO transition impact on valuation multiples
Energy Drink Market Growth
macro_topics.description: Energy drinks are taking a rising share of total beverages consumed globally, and retailers are allocating more premium cold shelf space to the category. Higher average selling prices relative to standard beverages make the category attractive for retailers, and cold placement drives impulse purchases — a structural tailwind for leading brands.

macro_topics.title: Tariff and Commodity Cost Pressure
macro_topics.description: The Iran conflict and Strait of Hormuz closure have elevated oil and commodity prices, feeding through to input costs for consumer brands. Celsius is experiencing near-term gross margin compression as a result, though management expects recovery above 50% gross margins once these pressures ease.

macro_topics.title: Consumer Staples Acquisitions
macro_topics.description: Celsius completed acquisitions of Alani Nu and A-Shoc, which inflate reported revenue growth to 33% in fiscal 2026 before normalizing to single digits in 2027–2028. M&A integration costs are depressing near-term margins but expanding total addressable market and distribution reach.

macro_topics.title: Buyback Programs as Value Signal
macro_topics.description: Management is repurchasing shares at prices well below Parkev's calculated fair value of $58. When insiders buy at a significant discount to intrinsic value, it signals confidence in the business trajectory and provides a floor of support for the stock price.
